data.section: Settlements, sustainability, transport, Heathrow and migration
data.sourceTitle: GCSE Geography Revision: Settlements and Urban Change
data.targetTitle: GCSE Geography Revision: Settlements and Urban Change
data.sourcePassage: This revision pack covers settlement site and situation, physical and human factors that shape settlements, modern settlement growth, sustainable cities, UK transport links, Heathrow Airport impacts and migration. Key settlement factors include wet point sites, dry point sites, defence, bridging points, fertile soil, fuel supply, shelter, aspect, building materials and communication. Modern cities grow because of accessibility, jobs, services and migration, but rapid growth can create pressure on housing, transport, water and services. Sustainable living reduces use of natural resources through renewable energy, public transport, efficient buildings, recycling and rainwater collection. Heathrow affects nearby settlements through jobs, transport links, noise, pollution, house prices and possible expansion impacts. Migration can be voluntary or forced, shaped by push and pull factors, with impacts on people, places and services.
data.targetPassage: This revision pack covers settlement site and situation, physical and human factors that shape settlements, modern settlement growth, sustainable cities, UK transport links, Heathrow Airport impacts and migration. Key settlement factors include wet point sites, dry point sites, defence, bridging points, fertile soil, fuel supply, shelter, aspect, building materials and communication. Modern cities grow because of accessibility, jobs, services and migration, but rapid growth can create pressure on housing, transport, water and services. Sustainable living reduces use of natural resources through renewable energy, public transport, efficient buildings, recycling and rainwater collection. Heathrow affects nearby settlements through jobs, transport links, noise, pollution, house prices and possible expansion impacts. Migration can be voluntary or forced, shaped by push and pull factors, with impacts on people, places and services.

questions.question: What is the best definition of a settlement site?
options: The actual land or place where a settlement is built
The surrounding connections of a settlement to other places
The number of shops in a settlement
The age of the settlement
questions.modelAnswer: The actual land or place where a settlement is built.

questions.question: What is the best definition of settlement situation?
options: The type of soil under a settlement
The location of a settlement in relation to surrounding places and routes
The shape of the settlement only
The number of houses in a settlement
questions.modelAnswer: The location of a settlement in relation to surrounding places and routes.

questions.question: Why might a settlement grow near route convergence?
options: Routes meeting can increase trade, communication and movement
The land is always colder there
It prevents all flooding
It guarantees fertile soil
questions.modelAnswer: Route convergence can increase trade, communication and movement, encouraging growth.

questions.question: Which answer best explains why modern settlements value accessibility?
options: People need access to services, work, shops and transport links
Modern settlements do not need resources
Accessibility only matters in rural areas
It only affects the weather
questions.modelAnswer: Accessibility helps people reach services, jobs, shops and transport.

questions.question: What is a wet point site?
options: A settlement close to a good water supply
A settlement far away from all rivers
A settlement built only on mountain tops
A settlement with no risk of rain
questions.modelAnswer: A wet point site is close to a good water supply.

questions.question: What is a dry point site?
options: A settlement built away from flood risk or wet ground
A settlement built inside a river channel
A place with no transport links
A settlement that must be on the coast
questions.modelAnswer: A dry point site is built away from flood risk or wet ground.

questions.question: Which factor means a settlement is built on higher ground for protection?
options: Defence
Fuel supply
Aspect
Wet point site
questions.modelAnswer: Defence means building on higher ground for protection.

questions.question: Why were building materials important for early settlements?
options: People needed local wood or stone to construct homes and other buildings
They made rivers easier to cross
They stopped all migration
They replaced the need for food
questions.modelAnswer: Local materials such as wood or stone made construction easier.

questions.question: Which factor is shown by “settlements built on slopes facing the sun”?
options: Aspect
Communication
Urbanisation
Forced migration
questions.modelAnswer: Aspect refers to the direction a slope faces, such as towards the sun.

questions.question: Why is fertile soil an important settlement factor?
options: It supports farming and food production
It creates airports automatically
It prevents traffic congestion
It removes the need for water
questions.modelAnswer: Fertile soil supports farming and food production.

questions.question: What is a bridging point?
options: A place where a river can be crossed or bridged easily
A place where no roads can meet
A settlement with no water supply
A slope facing away from the sun
questions.modelAnswer: A bridging point is where a river can be crossed or bridged easily.

questions.question: A village is on high ground, near woodland, and above the floodplain. Which three factors best explain its site?
options: Defence, fuel/building materials, and dry point site
Urbanisation, migration, and pollution
House prices, airports, and railways
Aspect, forced migration, and brain drain
questions.modelAnswer: Its site is explained by defence, access to wood/building materials, and being away from flood risk.

questions.question: The Lagos graph in the source shows population change over time. What is the main trend?
options: Rapid population growth
A steady decrease
No change
A sudden fall after 2000
questions.modelAnswer: The graph shows rapid population growth in Lagos.

questions.question: Which phrase best describes urban change visible using satellite images over time?
options: Changes in buildings, roads, land use and settlement extent
Changes in people’s names
Changes in the language spoken only
Changes in map colours only
questions.modelAnswer: Satellite images can show changes in buildings, roads, land use and settlement extent.

questions.question: Which factor can help explain why a city such as Lagos grows quickly?
options: Jobs, services and migration from rural areas
A complete lack of transport
No housing demand
Fewer economic opportunities
questions.modelAnswer: Jobs, services and rural-to-urban migration can contribute to rapid city growth.

questions.question: Why can rapid urban population growth create planning challenges?
options: It can increase pressure on housing, transport, water and services
It always reduces demand for resources
It means no new infrastructure is needed
It removes environmental impacts
questions.modelAnswer: Rapid growth can create pressure on housing, transport, water and services.

questions.question: What is urbanisation?
options: The increase in the proportion of people living in towns and cities
The decrease in all city populations
The building of only rural farms
The process of replacing renewable energy with coal
questions.modelAnswer: Urbanisation is the increase in the proportion of people living in towns and cities.

questions.question: What are non-renewable resources?
options: Resources that will eventually run out and cannot be replaced quickly
Resources that naturally replace themselves quickly
Resources that only exist in cities
Resources that cannot be used by people
questions.modelAnswer: Non-renewable resources will eventually run out and cannot be replaced quickly.

questions.question: Which is an example of a renewable resource?
options: Solar energy
Coal
Oil
Natural gas
questions.modelAnswer: Solar energy is renewable because it can be naturally replaced and will not run out in the same way as fossil fuels.

questions.question: What does sustainable living mean?
options: Living in a way that reduces the amount of natural resources used
Using as many resources as possible today
Avoiding all technology
Building only with concrete
questions.modelAnswer: Sustainable living means reducing the natural resources we use.

questions.question: What does sustainability mean?
options: Meeting today’s needs without harming future generations’ ability to meet their needs
Using all resources before future generations can use them
Only protecting businesses
Stopping all transport
questions.modelAnswer: Sustainability means meeting present needs without harming future generations.

questions.question: Which city design feature would reduce water demand from mains supply?
options: Rainwater collection
More petrol cars
Removing insulation
More landfill
questions.modelAnswer: Rainwater collection can reduce demand for treated mains water.

questions.question: How can green roofs make a city more sustainable?
options: They can improve insulation, absorb rainwater and support habitats
They increase fossil fuel use
They prevent any public transport
They make recycling impossible
questions.modelAnswer: Green roofs can improve insulation, absorb rainwater and support habitats.

questions.question: Which combination best fits a sustainable future city design?
options: Solar panels, cycling routes, insulation, recycling and rainwater collection
Coal power, car dependency, poor insulation, landfill and wasted water
More runways, more noise, less public transport and no recycling
No green spaces, no water saving and no renewable energy
questions.modelAnswer: A sustainable city would use renewable energy, low-carbon transport, efficient buildings, recycling and water saving.

questions.question: According to the UK train network task, what do train routes tend to connect?
options: Big cities
Only small villages
Only farms
Only airports
questions.modelAnswer: The train network tends to connect big cities.

questions.question: Why do many UK rail routes appear to radiate from London?
options: London is a major economic, political and transport centre
London has no need for commuters
No one travels to London
Railways avoid capital cities
questions.modelAnswer: Many routes radiate from London because it is a major economic, political and transport centre.

questions.question: Which factor best explains where transport links are usually located?
options: High demand between settlements and important services
Random choice with no link to population
Only places with no jobs
Only mountain summits
questions.modelAnswer: Transport links usually follow high demand between settlements and services.

questions.question: Why might transport networks be denser in south-east England than in remote upland areas?
options: Higher population density and stronger economic activity increase demand
Upland areas always have more commuters
Railways are never affected by relief
Population density has no effect
questions.modelAnswer: Higher population density and economic activity increase demand for transport links.

questions.question: Which nearby settlements are named in the Heathrow project?
options: Harmondsworth, Sipson and Harlington
Manchester, Leeds and York
Cardiff, Swansea and Newport
Oxford, Cambridge and Ely
questions.modelAnswer: The project names Harmondsworth, Sipson and Harlington as nearby settlements.

questions.question: Which issue is an environmental impact of Heathrow?
options: Pollution
More trumpet lessons
Less need for maps
No effect on wildlife
questions.modelAnswer: Pollution is an environmental impact of Heathrow.

questions.question: Which is an economic advantage of Heathrow for local settlements?
options: Jobs and business opportunities
Less access to transport
No visitors or trade
Fewer services
questions.modelAnswer: Heathrow can create jobs and business opportunities.

questions.question: Which is a social disadvantage of Heathrow expansion for local residents?
options: Noise disturbance and possible loss of homes
More renewable resources only
No change to daily life
Guaranteed lower traffic
questions.modelAnswer: Expansion can create noise disturbance and may threaten homes.

questions.question: Why might house prices near a major airport be affected?
options: Noise, air pollution, transport access and demand can influence desirability
Airports make every house identical in value
House prices are never linked to location
Only soil fertility affects house prices
questions.modelAnswer: Noise, pollution, accessibility and demand can influence house prices near airports.

questions.question: Which answer gives a balanced judgement about a third runway?
options: It may create jobs and improve capacity, but could increase noise, pollution and pressure on local communities
It has only advantages and no disadvantages
It has only disadvantages and no advantages
It is unrelated to local settlements
questions.modelAnswer: A balanced judgement recognises economic benefits but also environmental and social costs.

questions.question: What are pull factors?
options: Reasons that attract people to a new area
Reasons that force everyone to stay at home
Effects that only happen to wildlife
Maps showing train routes
questions.modelAnswer: Pull factors are reasons that attract people to a new area.

questions.question: What are push factors?
options: Reasons that drive people to leave their home
Reasons that attract tourists only
Benefits of recycling only
A type of train network
questions.modelAnswer: Push factors are reasons that drive people to leave their home.

questions.question: What is voluntary migration?
options: When people choose to move to a new place
When people are forced to leave because of conflict
When a river floods a settlement
When a railway line is built
questions.modelAnswer: Voluntary migration is when people choose to move to a new place.

questions.question: What is forced migration?
options: When people are forced to leave due to danger, conflict, persecution or natural disasters
When people choose to move for a better lifestyle
When people move only for holidays
When people build a new railway
questions.modelAnswer: Forced migration is when people are forced to leave due to danger, conflict, persecution or natural disasters.

questions.question: Which is the best example of a pull factor?
options: Job opportunities and safety
War and natural disaster
Flooding and persecution
Lack of jobs and conflict
questions.modelAnswer: Job opportunities and safety are pull factors because they attract people to a new area.

questions.question: Which set includes possible impacts of migration named in the source?
options: Economic growth, cultural exchange, brain drain, social tension and pressure on public services
Only better weather and no social change
Only reduced population everywhere
No effect on jobs, housing or culture
questions.modelAnswer: Possible impacts include economic growth, cultural exchange, brain drain, social tension and pressure on public services.
